rent.note: furnished 1BR in San Blas/Wanchaq; range $350–$600; altitude limits supply

utilities.note: electric + water + internet; $120–$200 range; heating required at 3,400m altitude

groceries.note: home cooking; local markets (San Pedro) very affordable

diningOut.note: local menus $2–4; tourist restaurants $10–20; mix essential

coworking.note: hot-desk monthly; growing scene; several hostels offer work spaces

simData.note: Claro/Movistar 30-day plan; coverage good in city

misc.note: gym, toiletries, entertainment, treks, excursions to ruins, buffer

accomSeasonality._doc: 12 monthly multipliers for RENT ONLY (Jan..Dec). 1.0 = base. Dry season (May–Oct) is tourist high season; rainy season lower demand.

evidence.claim: monthly costs $1,000–$1,400/month for nomad lifestyle in Cusco
evidence.quote: In Cusco, digital nomads can expect monthly costs of $1,000-1,400/month for a similar lifestyle

evidence.claim: rent $350–$600/month; utilities $120–$200 due to heating needs at altitude
evidence.quote: Rent in Cusco is relatively cheaper, ranging from $350-600 per month. Bills in Cusco are $120-$200 a month due to the higher altitude and cold nights requiring heating

evidence.claim: low-end budget ~$798/month with hostel accommodation including coworking
evidence.quote: a low-end budget in Cusco should expect around $798 USD per month total, with accommodation at $248 USD (in the Nomad Hideout, which includes coworking access)
$10 USD per day ($300 USD a month) for food, assuming street food, cooking, and local market purchases; $50 USD for transport
